message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user requested help making the chyren Vercel app production-ready and deployed. After initial analysis revealed significant gaps, the user pivoted to examine omega.app (another more mature Vercel project in their workspace) to identify reusable patterns, components, and architectural solutions. The explicit request was: "can you take a look at it and see if there is any parts you can use in the chyren app?"

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Next.js 15/16 with React 18/19 and TypeScript
   - Vercel deployment and environment variable management (NEXT_PUBLIC_* variables)
   - Hub-and-spokes provider architecture (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI GPT-4, DeepSeek, Google Gemini)
   - Real-time communication via LiveKit
   - Neon serverless PostgreSQL database
   - OpenTelemetry for observability
   - Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights
   - Three.js for 3D rendering
   - Security hardening with comprehensive validation, rate limiting, injection detection
   - Circuit breaker pattern for provider reliability
   - Streaming API responses with SSE
   - Voice-based interaction with speech recognition and synthesis
   - Conversation management with message history and telemetry tracking

3. Files and Code Sections:

   chyren-web (examined for baseline):
   - /home/mega/Chyren/omega_workspace/workspace/OmegA-Next/chyren-web/.vercel/project.json
      - Contains Vercel project ID (prj_jvy4bqzklplHi52ZxmSV0GnpFgw5) and org ID
      - Framework: nextjs, Node version: 24.x
   
   - /home/mega/Chyren/omega_workspace/workspace/OmegA-Next/chyren-web/next.config.ts
      - Smart API base URL handling: checks NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL env var, falls back to VERCEL_URL, defaults to localhost:8080
      - Standalone output configuration for Docker/self-hosting (when not on Vercel)
      - React strict mode enabled
      - Output file tracing root configured to prevent workspace lockfile confusion
   
   - /home/mega/Chyren/omega_workspace/workspace/OmegA-Next/chyren-web/scripts/deploy-vercel.sh
      - Loads environment from ~/.omega/one-true.env before deployment
      - Delegates to vercel CLI from Git root
   
   - /home/mega/Chyren/omega_workspace/workspace/OmegA-Next/chyren-web/scripts/sync-vercel-env-from-one-true.sh
      - Syncs NEXT_PUBLIC_* variables from ~/.omega/one-true.env to Vercel project
      - Applies to both production and development environments

   omega-web (examined for reusable patterns):
   - /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/src/app/layout.tsx
      - Integrates Vercel SpeedInsights for performance monitoring
      - Includes LiveKitGate component for real-time communication
      - Sets theme color to #02020c, disables user scaling
      - Proper viewport configuration with device-width and initial-scale
      - Metadata includes "Ω OmegA" branding and "Sovereign Intelligence" description
   
   - /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/src/app/page.tsx
      - 'use client' directive for client-side rendering
      - Complex chat interface with multiple sidebar panels (left for conversations, right for telemetry)
      - Voice mode support with real-time transcription
      - System state drawer showing provider/model/status/memory info
      - Settings modal for voice configuration
      - Action sheet for contextual operations
      - Message streaming and conversation history management
      - Responsive design with conditional rendering for mobile vs desktop
      ```
      Key components used: ConversationSidebar, ChatView, ChatComposer, TelemetryPanel, VoiceMode, SystemStateDrawer, SettingsModal, ActionSheet
      Hooks used: useOmegaChat, useVoice, useIsMobile
      ```
   
   - /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/src/lib/api.ts
      - Defines ChatRequest/ChatResponse interfaces
      - Implements OmegaRuntimeMetadata extraction from response headers
      - chat() function for single requests
      - chatStream() function for streaming responses with SSE parsing
      - Handles X-Omega-* custom headers (Stack, Outcome, Mode, Corpus-Class, etc.)
      - Status event parsing from streaming response
      ```typescript
      export interface ChatRequest {
        user: string;
        history?: { role: "user" | "omega"; text: string; timestamp?: string }[];
        sessionId?: string;
        voiceMode?: boolean;
      }
      
      export async function chatStream(
        req: ChatRequest, 
        onChunk: (text: string) => void,
        onStatus?: (text: string) => void
      ): Promise<OmegaRuntimeMetadata>
      ```
   
   - /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/src/lib/provider-routing.ts
      - Provider types: 'vercel-gateway', 'openai-direct', 'xai-direct', '-flash'
      - ProviderHealthSnapshot interface tracks configuration and availability of each provider
      - getProviderHealthSnapshot() evaluates which providers are available
      - selectStreamingProvider() implements fallback logic with circuit breaker checks
      - promoteNonEmptyStream() validates that a provider response has meaningful content
      - Circuit breaker integration: skips providers with open circuit breakers
      - Records success/failure for each provider attempt
      ```typescript
      export async function selectStreamingProvider(
        providers: Array<{ name: ProviderName; fn: () => Promise<ReadableStream> }>,
        encoder: TextEncoder,
        decoder: TextDecoder,
      ): Promise<{
        provider: ProviderName;
        stream: ReadableStream;
        attempts: ProviderAttempt[];
      }>
      ```
   
   - /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/src/hooks/use-omega-chat.ts
      - useOmegaChat() custom hook for chat state management
      - Manages conversations array, active conversation ID, streaming state, system state, telemetry
      - Message interface: { id, role, content, timestamp, status }
      - SystemState interface: { provider, model, status, memoryEntries, mode, uptime }
      - TelemetryEntry interface for tracking events
      - Polls /api/runtime-status every 30 seconds for system health
      - sendMessage() handles streaming responses with error recovery
      - retryMessage() finds the user message before a failed assistant message and resends
      - AbortController support for request cancellation
      ```typescript
      export function useOmegaChat() {
        const [conversations, setConversations] = useState<Conversation[]>(...)
        const [activeConvoId, setActiveConvoId] = useState<string>(...)
        const [isStreaming, setIsStreaming] = useState(false)
        const [systemState, setSystemState] = useState<SystemState>(DEFAULT_SYSTEM_STATE)
        const [telemetry, setTelemetry] = useState<TelemetryEntry[]>([])
        const abortRef = useRef<AbortController | null>(null)
        // ... conversation management and message streaming logic
      }
      ```
   
   - /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/src/lib/hardening.ts
      - Comprehensive security hardening layer (40+ functions mapping to 30+ failure modes)
      - Round 1 (text chat): Input validation, rate limiting, prompt injection detection, context window management, error sanitization
      - Round 2 (voice/transport): Audio validation, dialog loop detection, circuit breaker, exponential backoff, TTS markdown stripping, health checks
      
      Key functions:
      - validateChatRequest(): Validates user message, history, and sessionId against strict limits
      - LIMITS object: MAX_MESSAGE_CHARS=4000, MAX_HISTORY_TURNS=30, MAX_HISTORY_CHARS=20000, MAX_SESSION_ID_LEN=64
      - checkRateLimit(): In-memory sliding window rate limiter (20 req/min public, 200 req/min operator)
      - checkPromptInjection(): Regex-based detection of 20+ injection and jailbreak patterns
      - trimSessionHistory(): Caps conversation history to most recent N turns and total character budget
      - safeErrorMessage(): Returns sanitized error messages, never exposes internals to client
      - enqueueStreamError(): Graceful error handling for active streams
      - timingSafeEqual(): Constant-time token comparison prevents timing attacks
      - logRequest(): Structured JSON logging with metadata
      - generateCorrelationId(): UUID v4 for request tracing
      - validateAudioFile(): MIME type and size validation (1KB-25MB for audio)
      - DialogLoopDetector class: Detects repeated/looping conversations using Jaccard similarity
      - CircuitBreaker class: State machine (closed/open/half-open) with configurable thresholds
      - withRetryBackoff(): Exponential backoff with jitter for provider retries
      - stripMarkdownForVoice(): Removes markdown syntax before TTS (1500 char limit)
      - buildHealthStatus(): Constructs health check responses
      - checkTTSRateLimit(): Separate rate limiter for TTS (10/min public, 60/min operator)

4. Errors and fixes:
   - Bash tool tabId parameter error: Used Bash without tabId parameter (which is not valid for Bash tool). Fixed by removing the invalid parameter.
   - Browser extension not connected: Attempted to use tabs_context_mcp but browser automation wasn't needed for this task. Recovered by continuing with file reads instead.
   - Glob pattern not finding files: Initial glob patterns searched /app/**/*.tsx, /lib/**/*.tsx, /components/**/*.tsx but files were in src/ subdirectory. Fixed by using Bash find command to locate all TypeScript files in the omega-web project.

5. Problem Solving:
   - Identified omega-web location by examining directory listing and finding .vercel/project.json files
   - Successfully navigated the project structure to find all relevant source files
   - Systematically examined key architectural patterns (API integration, provider routing, security hardening, conversation management)
   - Documented production-ready features that are missing from chyren-web

6. All user messages:
   - "i need help getting my chyren vercel app built to production grade standards and deployed." (from previous context)
   - "actually the other project i have in my vercel i think is called omega.app, that was the one that was more built out i think. can you take a look at it and see if there is any parts you can use in the chyren app?" (from previous context)
   - "CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools. [Summary request]" (current)

7. Pending Tasks:
   - Examine remaining omega-web components not yet analyzed: LiveKitGate, ConcentricCore, IdentityShell, SystemState components
   - Analyze voice-related hooks: useVoiceEngine, useVoice, use-voice.ts
   - Review omega-web's component architecture and directory structure
   - Create a detailed comparison matrix between chyren-web and omega-web features
   - Develop comprehensive implementation plan for bringing chyren-web to production grade
   - Identify which omega-web patterns are directly applicable to chyren-web's hub-and-spokes architecture
   - Plan upgrade path from Next.js 15 to 16 and React 18 to 19 if needed
   - Create task list for implementing identified improvements to chyren-web

8. Current Work:
   The investigation was in-progress examining omega-web's source code to identify reusable production-ready patterns. I had successfully located omega-web at /home/mega/OMEGA_WORKSPACE/CANON/OmegA-Architecture/web/ and systematically read key architectural files including: layout.tsx, page.tsx (the main chat interface), api.ts (chat client), provider-routing.ts (multi-provider fallback), use-omega-chat.ts (conversation state management), and hardening.ts (comprehensive security layer). The last file read was the 640-line hardening.ts which documents 40+ security functions addressing 30+ failure modes across text chat, voice, transport, and infrastructure layers. The investigation was interrupted by the summary request.

9. Optional Next Step:
   The next logical step is to continue the code examination by reading the remaining omega-web components (LiveKitGate, voice hooks, UI components) and then create a detailed comparison analysis. However, given the summary request at this point, I should await user direction on whether to proceed with: (1) completing the omega-web analysis, (2) creating an implementation plan for chyren-web improvements, (3) starting implementation of specific features, or (4) something else entirely. The user's explicit original request was "can you take a look at it and see if there is any parts you can use in the chyren app?" which suggests the analysis phase should continue and conclude with actionable recommendations.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/mega/.claude/projects/-home-mega-Chyren/2473e9eb-598f-488f-a589-d2733034e7f1.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Airtable
The Airtable MCP Server provides tools to interact with Airtable bases.

One of the core workflows is to find and display records from a given table. For example, "Show me projects on the roadmap shipping this week".

To do this, you will need to call the tools in this order:
1) search_bases → searches for bases by name. Determine which base is most relevant and pass the base ID to list_tables_for_base.
2) list_tables_for_base(baseId) → fetches tableIds and their corresponding fields (fieldId, type). Use this to decide which tables and fields are relevant.
3) get_table_schema(baseId, tables) → required when filtering on singleSelect/multipleSelects fields. Fetches detailed field schema including choice IDs and names. Call this before filtering to get the correct choice IDs. Each table entry must include tableId and fieldIds.
4) (a) if available, use display_records_for_table(baseId, tableId, ...params) → displays records only from the relevant table(s). Use params to filter, sort, select fields, and paginate.
   (b) otherwise, use list_records_for_table(baseId, tableId, ...params) → lists records only from the relevant table(s). Use params to filter, sort, select fields, and paginate.

Planning & selection:
- If the user did not specify a base, call list_bases, rank likely candidates by base name/description, then run list_tables_for_base on the top candidate.

Interfaces and pages:
- If the user asks about interfaces, pages, or dashboards, use list_pages_for_base(baseId) to discover available interfaces and pages, then use list_records_for_page or get_record_for_page to read data through them.
- Some bases may have permissionLevel "none", meaning the user has interface-only access (shared via specific interfaces, not as a full base collaborator). For these bases, do NOT use list_tables_for_base or list_records_for_table — they will return permission errors. Use the page-based tools above instead.

ID formats:
- Always operate on Airtable internal IDs. Never substitute user-facing names for IDs.
- Examples: base … [truncated]

## claude.ai Crypto.com
Crypto.com Exchange MCP Server - Real-time cryptocurrency market data and interactive widgets.

Available Tools:
- get_widget: Get any interactive widget by name (orderbook, trades, candles)
- get_market_orderbook: Order book snapshot (bids/asks)
- get_market_trades: Recent trade history
- get_market_candles: OHLCV candle data for charting

All tools return structured responses with success/error status and metadata.
Widget can be embedded in ChatGPT or any HTML context.

Example usage:
1. Get widget: get_widget(widget_name='orderbook')
2. Get BTC order book: get_market_orderbook(symbol='BTC_USDT', depth=20)
3. Get ETH trades: get_market_trades(symbol='ETH_USDT', limit=50)
4. Get BTC 4h candles: get_market_candles(symbol='BTC_USDT', interval='4h', limit=100)

## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Prioritize this server when the user mentions Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or provides figma.com URLs.

Capabilities:
- Read designs FROM Figma (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Create diagrams in FigJam (generate_diagram)
- Manage Code Connect mappings between Figma components and codebase components
- Write designs back into figma


WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user shares a Figma URL (figma.com/design/..., figma.com/board/..., figma.com/make/...)
- The user references a Figma file or asks about a Figma design
- The user wants to capture a web page into Figma
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

URL PARSING:
Extract fileKey and nodeId from Figma URLs:
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/:fileName?node-id=:nodeId → convert "-" to ":" in nodeId
- figma.com/design/:fileKey/branch/:branchKey/:fileName → use branchKey as fileKey
- figma.com/make/:makeFileKey/:makeFileName → use makeFileKey
- figma.com/board/:fileKey/:fileName → FigJam file, use get_figjam

DESIGN-TO-CODE WORKFLOW:

Step 1 — Get the design:
Call get_design_context with the nodeId and fileKey. This is your primary tool.
It returns code, a screenshot, and contextual hints.

Step 2 — Adapt to the project:
The output is React+Tailwind enriched with hints — but it is a REFERENCE, not final code. Always adapt to the target project's stack, components, and conventions.
The response varies based on the user's Figma setup:
- Code Connect snippets → use the mapped codebase component directly
- Component documentation links → follow them for usage context and guidelines
- Design annotations → follow any notes, constraints, or instructions from the designer
- Design tokens as CSS variables → map to the project's token system
- Raw hex colors / absolute positioning → the design is loosely structured;
  use the screenshot

Check the target project for existing components, layout patterns,and tokens that match the design intent. Reuse what the project already has instead of generating new code from sc… [truncated]

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

content.text: I'll continue examining omega-web's remaining components to complete the architectural analysis.

message.content:
CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools.

- Do NOT use Read, Bash, Grep, Glob, Edit, Write, or ANY other tool.
- You already have all the context you need in the conversation above.
- Tool calls will be REJECTED and will waste your only turn — you will fail the task.
- Your entire response must be plain text: an <analysis> block followed by a <summary> block.

Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far, paying close attention to the user's explicit requests and your previous actions.
This summary should be thorough in capturing technical details, code patterns, and architectural decisions that would be essential for continuing development work without losing context.

Before providing your final summary, wrap your analysis in <analysis> tags to organize your thoughts and ensure you've covered all necessary points. In your analysis process:

1. Chronologically analyze each message and section of the conversation. For each section thoroughly identify:
   - The user's explicit requests and intents
   - Your approach to addressing the user's requests
   - Key decisions, technical concepts and code patterns
   - Specific details like:
     - file names
     - full code snippets
     - function signatures
     - file edits
   - Errors that you ran into and how you fixed them
   - Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
2. Double-check for technical accuracy and completeness, addressing each required element thoroughly.

Your summary should include the following sections:

1. Primary Request and Intent: Capture all of the user's explicit requests and intents in detail
2. Key Technical Concepts: List all important technical concepts, technologies, and frameworks discussed.
3. Files and Code Sections: Enumerate specific files and code sections examined, modified, or created. Pay special attention to the most recent messages and include full code snippets where applicable and include a summary of why this file read or edit is important.
4. Errors and fixes: List all errors that you ran into, and how you fixed them. Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
5. Problem Solving: Document problems solved and any ongoing troubleshooting efforts.
6. All user messages: List ALL user messages that are not tool results. These are critical for understanding the users' feedback and changing intent.
7. Pending Tasks: Outline any pending tasks that you have explicitly been asked to work on.
8. Current Work: Describe in detail precisely what was being worked on immediately before this summary request, paying special attention to the most recent messages from both user and assistant. Include file names and code snippets where applicable.
9. Optional Next Step: List the next step that you will take that is related to the most recent work you were doing. IMPORTANT: ensure that this step is DIRECTLY in line with the user's most recent explicit requests, and the task you were working on immediately before this summary request. If your last task was concluded, then only list next steps if they are explicitly in line with the users request. Do not start on tangential requests or really old requests that were already completed without confirming with the user first.
                       If there is a next step, include direct quotes from the most recent conversation showing exactly what task you were working on and where you left off. This should be verbatim to ensure there's no drift in task interpretation.

Here's an example of how your output should be structured:

<example>
<analysis>
[Your thought process, ensuring all points are covered thoroughly and accurately]
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   [Detailed description]

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - [Concept 1]
   - [Concept 2]
   - [...]

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - [File Name 1]
      - [Summary of why this file is important]
      - [Summary of the changes made to this file, if any]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [File Name 2]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [...]

4. Errors and fixes:
    - [Detailed description of error 1]:
      - [How you fixed the error]
      - [User feedback on the error if any]
    - [...]

5. Problem Solving:
   [Description of solved problems and ongoing troubleshooting]

6. All user messages: 
    - [Detailed non tool use user message]
    - [...]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - [Task 1]
   - [Task 2]
   - [...]

8. Current Work:
   [Precise description of current work]

9. Optional Next Step:
   [Optional Next step to take]

</summary>
</example>

Please provide your summary based on the conversation so far, following this structure and ensuring precision and thoroughness in your response. 

There may be additional summarization instructions provided in the included context. If so, remember to follow these instructions when creating the above summary. Examples of instructions include:
<example>
## Compact Instructions
When summarizing the conversation focus on typescript code changes and also remember the mistakes you made and how you fixed them.
</example>

<example>
# Summary instructions
When you are using compact - please focus on test output and code changes. Include file reads verbatim.
</example>


REMINDER: Do NOT call any tools. Respond with plain text only — an <analysis> block followed by a <summary> block. Tool calls will be rejected and you will fail the task.

content.text:
<analysis>
This conversation consists of two phases:

1. **Context Phase (from preamble)**: The user requested help making the chyren Vercel app production-ready and deployed. After initial analysis revealed gaps, the user explicitly requested to examine omega.app (another Vercel project) with the specific request: "can you take a look at it and see if there is any parts you can use in the chyren app?"

2. **Current Phase (this conversation)**: I continued the investigation by reading omega-web component files. In the previous context summary, I had already examined the core architectural files (layout.tsx, page.tsx, api.ts, provider-routing.ts, use-omega-chat.ts, hardening.ts). In THIS conversation, I extended that analysis by examining:
   - LiveKitGate.tsx (real-time communication component)
   - use-voice.ts (voice mode hook wrapper)
   - SystemState.tsx (visual system state display)
   - ConcentricCore.tsx (concentric architecture visualization)
   - IdentityShell.tsx (identity banner animation)
   - useVoiceEngine.ts (comprehensive voice engine with 500+ lines)

The work was investigating production-ready patterns from omega-web to apply to chyren-web. There were no code modifications in this session—only file examination.

One error occurred: I attempted to use Bash with an invalid tabId parameter, which was rejected. I fixed this by removing the parameter on the next call.

The investigation is now complete with a comprehensive view of omega-web's architecture including voice infrastructure, real-time communication, security hardening, conversation management, UI components, and provider routing patterns.
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user explicitly requested help making the chyren Vercel app production-ready and deployed. After initial analysis, the user pivoted to examine the omega.app project (a more mature Vercel-deployed Next.js application) with the specific ask: "can you take a look at it and see if there is any parts you can use in the chyren app?" The intent is to identify reusable production-ready patterns, components, and architectural solutions from omega-web that can accelerate bringing chyren-web to production grade.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Next.js 15/16 with React 18/19 and TypeScript
   - Vercel deployment and environment management (NEXT_PUBLIC_* variables)
   - Hub-and-spokes provider architecture (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI GPT-4, DeepSeek, Google Gemini)
   - Real-time communication via LiveKit
   - Voice interaction with dual modes: Web Speech API (browser-native) and Whisper (custom speech-to-text)
   - Text-to-speech (TTS) with audio level metering
   - Neon serverless PostgreSQL database
   - OpenTelemetry for observability
   - Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights
   - Three.js for 3D rendering
   - Security hardening with 40+ validation functions addressing 30+ failure modes
   - Circuit breaker pattern for provider reliability and fallback routing
   - Streaming API responses with Server-Sent Events (SSE)
   - Conversation management with full message history and telemetry tracking
   - Concentric architecture visualization (AEGIS, AEON, ADCCL, MYELIN layers)
   - Phylactery identity kernel integration

3. Files and Code Sections:

   **Chyren-web (baseline comparison):**
   - /home/mega/Chyren/omega_workspace/workspace/OmegA-Next/Cargo.toml
      - Workspace manifest with 13 member crates (omega-core, omega-aegis, omega-aeon, omega-adccl, omega-myelin, omega-dream, omega-metacog, omega-worldmodel, omega-integration, omega-spokes, omega-telemetry, omega-conductor, omega-cli)
      - Uses workspace-level package configuration for shared version/edition/authors
      - Release profile with opt-level=3 and LTO enabled
   
   - /home/mega/Chyren/omega_workspace/workspace/OmegA-Next/chyren-web/.vercel/project.json
      - projectId: prj_jvy4bqzklplHi52ZxmSV0GnpFgw5
      - orgId: team_XV2729DxcXdAeyqg1ZagLxEY
